coredata.dc:title: Construction of urchin-like bimetallic phosphides induced by carbon dots for efficient wide pH hydrogen production
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Haixing
Jiang, Xu
Jin, Mengjing
Song, Jianqiao
Li, Muxuan
Zhou, Jinyuan
Pan, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: The development of an efficient noble-metal-free and pH-universal electrocatalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) would be highly significant for hydrogen (H2) production via electrocatalytic water splitting. However, developing such a catalyst remains a formidable task. Herein, a strategy is proposed for the in situ fabrication of a novel urchin-like NiCoP microsphere catalyst (0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF) on nickel foam (NF) using carbon dots (CDs) as a directing agent. The strong bonding between the CDs and metals provides additional active sites, giving 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF excellent electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance in environments ranging from acidic to basic. Moreover, the unique structure of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF endows this catalyst with low Tafel slopes of 73, 146 and 74 mV dec−1 for HER in acidic, neutral and alkaline conditions, respectively. This performance exceeds that of numerous other reported non-precious HER catalysts. In summary, this work offers a novel and efficient strategy for the design and synthesis of low-cost, efficient, and robust transition metal phosphides (TMPs) electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic phosphides
Wide pH hydrogen evolution reaction
Carbon dots
Electrocatalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), (b) SEM images (inset: modeling diagram); (c)–(d) TEM images; (e) HRTEM; (f) EDS mapping of the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF.
SEM images of the (a), (d) NiCoP/NF; (b), (e) 0.2CDs-NiCoP/NF; (c), (f) 1.0CDs-NiCoP/NF (inset: modeling diagrams).

(a) XRD patterns of the Bare NF and 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF; (b) Raman spectra of the NiCoP/NF and XCDs-NiCoP/NF (X = 0.2, 0.5, 1.0); (c) overall XPS spectra, and high-resolution XPS spectra for (d) Ni 2p; (e) Co 2p; (f) P 2p of NiCoP/NF and 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF.

HER performances of the Bare NF, NiCoP/NF, XCDs-NiCoP/NF and Pt/C NF catalysts measured in 0.5 M H2SO4, 1 M PBS and 1 M KOH; (a)-(c) Polarization curves; (d)-(f) corresponding Tafel plots; (g)-(i) EIS Nyquist plots.

(a)-(c) Polarization curves of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF at initial and after 1000, 2000 CV cycles. Inset: Chronoamperometric j–t curve of the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF at a constant overpotential for 20 h, (d)-(f) XRD patterns of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF before and after 20 h HER test in 0. 5 M H2SO4, 1 M PBS and 1.0 M KOH.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic process for the NiCoP/NF and 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF.

head.ce:title: Construction of urchin-like bimetallic phosphides induced by carbon dots for efficient wide pH hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: New Energy Photovoltaic Industry Research Center, Qinghai University, Xining 810016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: New Energy Photovoltaic Industry Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: New Energy Photovoltaic Industry Research Center, Qinghai University, Xining 810016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: On the surface of nickel foam, carbon-doped bimetallic phosphide 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF is effectively prepared using a facile hydrothermal and phosphorization technique. CDs doping produces a distinct urchin-like sample with more exposed active sites, larger surface area, and faster charge transfer. Furthermore, the CDs-doped heteroatoms caused further electron transfer, all of which resulted in These advantages provide the created 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF with strong electrocatalytic capabilities and allow the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF to function well in hydrogen precipitation at a wide pH range.

The development of an efficient noble-metal-free and pH-universal electrocatalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) would be highly significant for hydrogen (H2) production via electrocatalytic water splitting. However, developing such a catalyst remains a formidable task. Herein, a strategy is proposed for the in situ fabrication of a novel urchin-like NiCoP microsphere catalyst (0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF) on nickel foam (NF) using carbon dots (CDs) as a directing agent. The strong bonding between the CDs and metals provides additional active sites, giving 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF excellent electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance in environments ranging from acidic to basic. Moreover, the unique structure of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF endows this catalyst with low Tafel slopes of 73, 146 and 74 mV dec−1 for HER in acidic, neutral and alkaline conditions, respectively. This performance exceeds that of numerous other reported non-precious HER catalysts. In summary, this work offers a novel and efficient strategy for the design and synthesis of low-cost, efficient, and robust transition metal phosphides (TMPs) electrocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its environmental friendliness and recyclability, hydrogen (H2) has been identified as one of the most promising potential alternatives to increasingly depleted conventional fossil fuels [1–3]. Water splitting is a simple and clean strategy for producing H2, and water electrolysis can be easily combined with renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar energy [4,5]. Consequently, efficient electrocatalysts that yield sufficient current densities at low overpotentials are urgently needed to enable large-scale H2 generation [6,7]. Among hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) catalysts, platinum (Pt) group metal compounds exhibit remarkable electrocatalytic activity However, the practical application of Pt group metal compounds is severely restricted by their high cost and scarcity [8–10]. Moreover, the HER activity of Pt group metal compounds is approximately two orders of magnitude lower under alkaline conditions compared with acidic conditions [11–14]. Therefore, developing a non-noble metal HER electrocatalyst as a cost-effective and highly active alternative electrocatalyst would be of great significance [15]. In recent years, transition metal-based electrocatalysts (such as chalcogenides [16,17], phosphides [10,15], nitrides [18], and carbides [19]) have been widely investigated due to their unique electronic structures and high activity.

In particular, efficient HER catalysts should exhibit good performance under all pH conditions, even though different media require different mechanisms [20–22]. Similar to Pt-based catalysts, most electrocatalysts in alkaline environments exhibit much slower HER kinetics compared to the same catalysts in acidic media. This kinetic difference is due to the different sources of the hydrogen intermediate (Had). Protons are readily available in acidic media, but under basic or neutral conditions, hydrogen is produced by the slow kinetics of water molecule dissociation, which requires specific active sites [23]. Since the groundbreaking work of Lewis and Schaak, transition metal phosphides (TMPs) have been widely used for catalyzing HER [24]. However, the HER performance of TMPs under neutral and basic conditions is always unsatisfactory. Consequently, to solve this problem, many attempts have been made to enhance the catalytic activity of TMPs via doping with exotic metallic or nonmetallic elements, constructing homogeneous/heterogeneous polymetallic phases, and architectural engineering [25]. Another challenge with TMP catalysts is that they are directly immobilized on the substrate, leading to poor adhesion and unsatisfactory mechanical durability during long-term use. This means that TMPs are unable to achieve the expected catalytic activity and durability in pH-universal conditions [15]. Some studies have demonstrated that bimetallic phosphides can modulate the electronic structure of monometal TMPs. This promotes the adsorption/desorption of intermediates at intermediate binding energies, increasing the endogenous activity of these active sites and leading to improve the electrocatalytic activity [26–28]. Unfortunately, bubble interference affects the active area of bimetallic phosphides resulting in insufficient of electron transfer capacity [29].

The hybridization of carbon nanomaterials and transition metal compounds results in the generation of unique interfaces, causing the exposure of more active sites and efficient electron transfer [30–32]. Thus, different carbon nanostructures can be combined with electrocatalysts to enhance their catalytic performance [33–36].As a new member of the carbon material family, carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm [37]. Due to their flexible composition, tunable electronic structure and excellent electrical conductivity, CDs have proven to be an inexpensive nanostructure suitable for hydrogen electrocatalysis [38]. Abundant surface/edge functional groups can act as active centers or provide bonding sites for the construction of CDs-metal materials, thus enhancing their charge transfer rate and stability [39,40]. Furthermore, the strong bonding between CDs and metals ensures effective CDs-metal interactions, which also provides many interfacial active sites for HER [41]. Doping CDs with heteroatoms can induce electronic structure changes and further improve electron transfer properties. Thus, doping is a beneficial strategy for improving electrocatalytic performance. Electronegativity differences between doping heteroatoms and C atoms, lead to effective charge transfer, and structural distortion is induced by adjacent C atoms [42,43]. These factors enhance the adsorption/desorption of intermediates on the catalyst surface, change the local density of states, adjust the electronic structure, and enhance the intrinsic activity of active sites [44,45]. More importantly, the negative charge on the surface of CDs can attract positive metal cations, control the growth of crystals and influence the structure of the composite [46].

To solve the problem of bubble interference in bimetallic phosphides affecting further catalytic processes, a feasible strategy for constructing a uniquely structured catalyst with enhanced conductivity and superhydrophilic/gasophobic properties using doped CDs is proposed. Herein, nickel foam (NF) was used as a self-supporting electrode material to prepare a novel surface-grown three-dimensional urchin-like catalyst (0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF) through a facile hydrothermal and phosphorization treatment by controlling the amount of N, P-doped CDs, which were used as a structure-directing agent. Due to its unique morphology, this catalyst possesses a large specific surface area and numerous active sites leading to efficient HER performance in a wide pH range. Impressively, the as-synthesized 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF requires overpotentials of just 117, 126, and 115 mV in acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Furthermore, after 1000 and 2000 cyclic voltammetry scans under different electrolytes or continuous electrolysis for 20 h, the current density of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF remains basically unchanged, demonstrating the excellent durability and long-term stability of this catalyst. The design strategy reported herein for the preparation of CDs-oriented bimetallic phosphide electrocatalysts may provide valuable new insight for the design of other non-noble metal pH-universal electrocatalysts.

First, the nickel foam (NF) was pretreated prior to catalyst synthesis. A 1 × 2 cm2 NF was sonicated with 3 M HCl, ethanol and deionized water for 20 min to remove the oxides and stains on the NF surface. The pre-treated NF was dried at 70 °C and set aside. The synthesis steps for preparing of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF are shown in Scheme 1 . One piece of pre-treated NF, 0.336 g C4H6CoO4·4H2O, 0.4 g urea, and 15 mg CDs (equal to 5 mg mL−1) were added to 30 mL of deionized water, thoroughly stirred, and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was then performed at 120 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the obtained sample was washed with deionized water and dried at 70 °C overnight. Subsequently, the dried sample and 0.5 g NaH2PO2 were loaded in a tube furnace (NaH2PO2 upstream, sample downstream), annealed at 300 °C under an argon atmosphere for 150 min, and cooled to room temperature, Next, the annealed sample was washed with deionized water until there were no impurities on the surface and dried at 70 °C. The final mass loadings of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF on NF are about 2.3 mg cm−2. Additional samples were prepared by varying the concentration of CDs (0, 0.2, 1 mg mL−1) with all other steps performed in the same way. Based on the CDs concentration, these catalysts were denoted NiCoP/NF, 0.2CDs-NiCoP/NF and 1.0CDs-NiCoP/NF.

Electrochemical testing of all samples was performed on an RST 5000 electrochemical workstation (Suzhou Risetest Instrument Co., Ltd.). For the three-electrode system, the sample obtained from the reaction was directly used as the working electrode (1 × 1 cm2), Hg/HgO electrode (1 M KOH) and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) (0.5 M H2SO4 and 1 M PBS) as the reference electrode, and the carbon rod as the counter electrode. The overpotential, measured against a SCE, was converted to the potential versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the equation Evs
RHE = E (Hg/HgO) + 0.098 + 0.059 pH = E (SCE) + 0.244 + 0.059 pH. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements for HER at scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The Tafel slope was obtained from polarization curve, and the result based on the Tafel equation (η = a + b × log|j|, η is for overpotential, a is for intercept, b is for Tafel slope, and j is for current density). The electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl ) of the catalysts can be calculated from a series of cyclic voltammogram (CV) tests at a scan rate (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 mV s−1), and the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the product can be obtained from the Cdl of the resulting product and the Cdl of the substrate. ECSA could be calculated from the following equation:
ECSA =
C
dl
/
C s
. For the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement, the frequency range was 0.001 to 100,000 Hz. All electrochemical measurements were performed without iR compensation.

The microscopic morphology of the CDs and catalysts was evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI TecnaiF30, operated at 200 kV) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S-4800). The chemical component analysis was carried out via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Thermo Nicolet 6700) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI-5702, Al Kα source (h = 1486.6 eV)). Fluorescence spectra of CDs was measured on an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a D/Max-2400 instrument in the 2θ range of 30° to 70°. Raman spectra were recorded on a WITec alpha 300 R confocal Raman spectrometer. Brunauer–Emmet–Teller (BET) specific surface areas were determined using an ASAP2020 Micromeritics instrument.
The synthesis and characterization of CDs are described in the Supporting Information.

A two-step process is used to prepare 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF, as depicted in Scheme 1. Urchin-like 0.5CDs-NiCo-CH/NF is generated on the surface of NF after hydrothermal treatment (Fig. S4), since the partial substitution of Co ions by Ni ions in 0.5CDs-NiCo-CH/NF does not change the crystal structure and only leads to a negligible change in the lattice parameters [47–49]. The generation of Co (CO3)0.5(OH) ∙ 0·.11H2O (PDF#70-0989) is a prerequisite for the further phosphorization synthesis of TMP. Upon phosphorization, the color of NF surface changes from gray to black without any impurities (Fig. S5), and obtained 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF with the same morphology as the precursor 0.5CDs-NiCo-CH/NF. Characterization and analysis of the morphology and structure of the as-prepared 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF are carried out on scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1 a, b, the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF shows several radial nanowires. This structure can provide a more active sites and enable better contact between catalytic sites and the electrolyte. Moreover, this nanowire structure tends to rupture larger H2 bubbles and keep them away from the electrode surface, as shown in the schematic diagram at the bottom-right corner of Scheme 1. This is crucial because transporting H2 bubbles from the electrode surface can ensure good contact at the solid–liquid interface, potentially boosting the HER performance of the catalyst [50,51]. The TEM images (Fig. 1c, d) further confirm the morphology of the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF. An HRTEM image of the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF tip is shown in Fig. 1e. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.22 nm is related to the (111) crystal plane of NiCoP crystal, and the 0.21 nm and 0.25 nm lattice fringe spacings correspond to those of the CDs. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping images in Fig. 1f also clearly show that the Co, Ni, and P elements are uniformly distributed in 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF.

To evaluate the formation of the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF structure and the role of the CDs within this system, a series of samples (NiCoP/NF, 0.2CDs-NiCoP/NF, and 1.0CDs-NiCoP/NF) were synthesized with different concentrations of CDs (0, 0.2, and 1.0 mg mL−1). Without the addition of CDs, a nanoflower morphology is observed (Fig. 2 a, d). However, upon the addition of CDs (0.2 mg mL−1), the initial formation of an urchin-like substance is generated (Fig. 2b, e). By increasing the CDs concentration to 0.5 mg mL−1, nanowires are formed on the surface, enhancing the formation of an urchin-like morphology. However, if the concentration of CDs is further increased to 1.0 mg mL−1, the growth of crystals along specific directions is significantly disrupted. This forces the formation of uneven spherical structures on the surface, thereby reducing the surface energy (Fig. 2c, f) [52]. These phenomena suggest that the abundant hydroxyl and carbon-based functional groups in CDs play a significant role controlling the morphology of the catalyst [53]. Therefore, it can be proposed that the hydroxyl and carbon-based functional groups contained in the CDs of this system have selective adsorption properties. The CDs can bind with Ni and Co ions, providing more more crystal nucleation sites. This is conducive to the directional anisotropic growth of newly formed crystal nuclei, ultimately leading to the growth of urchin-like structures [54].

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is used to characterize the crystal structure of the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF. This catalyst displays the same Ni diffraction peaks as those observed in the diffraction pattern of Bare NF at 44.5° and 51.8°. Moreover, diffraction peaks at 41°, 44.9°, 47.5°, 54.4°, and 55.3° correspond to the (111), (201), (210), (300), and (211) crystal planes of NiCoP (PDF#71–2336), and the diffraction peaks at 38.9° and 48.4° correspond to the (201) and (202) crystal planes of CoP (PDF#29-0497), respectively. The XRD patterns of the other samples are shown in Fig. S6. Similar to 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF, the other catalysts show small NiCoP (PDF#71-2336) diffraction peaks. Furthermore, the increasing addition of CDs leads to a slight leftward shift in the locations of the diffraction peaks. This is potentially because the doping of CDs increases the cell parameters of the samples and expands the crystal spacing at the surface. This corresponds to the appearance of the larger 0.25 nm lattice spacing in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1c). Raman spectra for the as-synthesized catalysts were obtained by exciting the samples at 532 nm, as shown in Fig. 3b. The Raman spectra of the catalysts mainly show peaks attributed to C and NiCoP, confirming the successful doping of CDs in NiCoP/NF. The peaks at about 600 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 are the characteristic Raman peaks of NiCoP [55]. The peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1 are the typical D (disordered/defective sp3 carbon) and G (crystalline graphite sp2 carbon) peaks of the carbon material, respectively. These peaks demonstrate the presence of graphitic carbon [22], and the successful doping of CDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of NiCoP/NF and 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF are described and studied to evaluate the chemical state of the catalyst before and after doping with CDs. As shown in Fig. 3c, the survey spectra verify the presence of P, O, Co, and Ni elements on the surface of NiCoP/NF and 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF. It should be noted that the presence of O element in the XPS spectra of these TMP catalysts may be due to the oxidation of TMP surface in the air [56–58]. The high-resolution Ni 2p spectra (Fig. 3f) show six peaks correspond to Ni-P (852.7 and 870.0 eV), oxidized Ni species (856.4 and 873.7 eV), and satellite peaks (861.9 and 879.2 eV) [59]. The high-resolution Co 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 3e) show peaks located at 778.5 and 793.9, 782 and 797.4, 786.2 and 801.6 eV corresponding to Co-P, oxidized Co species, and satellite peaks, respectively [60]. In the high-resolution P 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 3f), the peaks of 129.4, 130.2, and134.2 eV that can be attributed to the P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 orbitals of phosphides (Pδ−) and the oxidized P species (PO), respectively. Compared to the Co 2p and P 2p spectra of NiCoP/NF, those of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF are negatively shifted by 0.5 and 0.2 eV, respectively. This indicates that the electron density in NiCoP increases after CD doping [61]. Additionally, the partial positive charges (δ+) of Ni, Co, and P ions and the partial negative charges (δ−) of P ions suggest the creation of electronic structures such as hydrogenases [62], M (δ+) and P (δ−) act as hydride receptors and proton receptor centers, favoring the HER process [63].

The outstanding physicochemical and electrocatalytic characteristics of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF described in Section 3.1 suggest that this catalyst may be suitable for operating in a wide pH range for HER. For the sustainable development of diverse renewable technologies, HER electrocatalysts with outstanding catalytic activity at all pH values are essential [64]. Therefore, the HER performance of the prepared catalysts was tested in 0.5 M H2SO4, 1 M PBS, and 1 M KOH. As a self-supporting electrode, 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF can be directly used as a working electrode in HER testing, and the HER performance of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF was compared to that of Bare NF, NiCoP/NF, 0.2CDs-NiCoP/NF, 1.0CDs-NiCoP/NF and Pt/C NF under the same conditions. The LSV polarization curves of Bare NF, NiCoP/NF, XCDs-NiCoP/NF (X = 0.2, 0.5, 1.0), and Pt/C NF in different electrolytes are shown in Fig. 4 a–c. As shown in Fig. S7, 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF has excellent HER performance under the three pH conditions, requiring overpotentials (denoted as η) of only 117 (0.5 M H2SO4), 126 (1 M PBS), 115 mV (1 M KOH) to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively. These overpotentials are much lower than those of NiCoP/NF (η = 195, 164, 198 mV) and close to those of Pt/C NF (η = 109, 80, 76 mV). Moreover, these results demonstrate that HER activity does not linearly rise with increasing CDs concentration. Rather, the optimum CDs concentration is 0.5 mg mL−1, and higher excessive CDs concentrations have a negative impact on HER activity. The Tafel slopes of the catalysts were obtained to further evaluate their HER performance, and kinetic process. In different electrolytes, 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF exhibits Tafel slopes of 73 (0.5 M H2SO4), 146 (1 M PBS), 74 mV dec−1 (1 M KOH), as displayed in Fig. 4d-f. A Tafel slope between 40 and 120 mV dec−1 indicates that HER proceeds via the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism, while a slope between 30 and 40 mV dec−1 indicates the Volmer-Tafel mechanism [65]. Therefore the slopes of the 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF (the slope in neutral media is approximately 120 mV dec−1) indicate that HER follows a typical Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism (
H 2
O +
e
-
→
H
a d s
+
O H
-
,
H
2
O +
H
a d s
→
H 2
( g )
+
O H
-
) at the surface of this catalyst [66]. Moreover, the smaller Tafel slope suggests a more rapid charge transfer kinetics.

Kapitha Fig. S8a-e shows the CV curves of the Bare NF, NiCoP/NF and XCDs-NiCoP/NF samples at different scan rates. These curves were used to determine their Cdl values (Fig. S8f). Among them, 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF presents the highest Cdl of 48.84 mF cm−2. This value is significantly higher than that of Bare NF (1.02 mF cm−2) and nearly 2.71 times higher than that of NiCoP/NF (17.97 mF cm−2), As determined by nitrogen (N2) adsorption–desorption measurements (Fig. S9a), 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF (4.144 m2 g−1) also has a larger specific surface area (SSA) than NiCoP/NF (2.600 m2 g−1). These indicate that the urchin-like 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF catalyst has more exposed active surface sites and a larger contact area with the electrolyte compared to the other samples. However, 1.0CDs-NiCoP/NF exhibits a lower Cdl value of 19.67 mF cm−2, indicating that excessive CDs content causes the active sites of NiCoP to be covered. Thus, the optimum CDs content is required to effectively improve the electronic structure and morphology of the catalyst [54]. The turnover frequency (TOF) of HER is related to the number of H2 molecules generated per second per active site, and this value is an essential indicator for understanding the intrinsic activity of an HER electrocatalyst [67]. As shown in Fig. S11, the TOF of the catalysts increases with increasing overpotential. Fig. S12 clearly shows that at η = 200 mV, the TOF values of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF in different media are 1.6 (0.5 M H2SO4), 1.27 (1 M PBS), and 1.67 s−1 (1 M KOH). These values are an average of 1.65 times higher than those of NiCoP/NF (0.95, 0.94, and 0.87 s−1). Therefore, in addition to the larger contact area and greater number of exposed active surface sites, the excellent HER activity of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF is also improved by its enhanced intrinsic activity.

To further analyze the HER kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface, electrochemical impedance analysis (EIS) was performed to obtain Nyquist diagrams. An equivalent circuit can be created by connecting two parallel resistors (Rs and Rct ) in series with a constant-phase element (CPE) in parallel using a Nyquist diagram. Rs represents the ohmic resistance and contact resistance of the electrolyte, and Rct represents the charge transfer resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface (Fig. S13a-c inset) [68]. The EIS curves of the Bare NF, NiCoP/NF, XCDs-NiCoP/NF and Pt/C NF are shown in Fig. 4g-i. 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF exhibits a smaller semicircle diameter than most of the other catalysts, and its diameter is similar to that of Pt/C NF. Thus, 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF has a smaller Rct (Table S2) and higher electron transfer rate than the other prepared catalysts [69]. This value is much lower than that of NiCoP/NF and Bare NF, which indicates that 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF has the strongest charge transfer performance.

To ensure a long catalyst lifetime and low hydrogen production costs, good HER catalysts require excellent stability. To evaluate the stability and durability of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF, polarization curves were measured after 1000 and 2000 consecutive CV cycles (Fig. 5 a–c). These polarization curves show that 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF exhibits a negligible loss in current density in different electrolytes. In addition, 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF shows a constant current density response for 20 h in a chronoamperometric stability test (Fig. 5a–c inset). The 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF catalyst was characterized after the 20 h chronoamperometric stability test to evaluate any potential changes. XRD analysis shows that the crystal structure of this catalyst does not significantly change (Fig. 5d–f). The SEM images do show slight morphological changes compared to the pre-test 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF, but the original unique structure of the catalyst is still maintained (Fig. S14, S15a–c). These results indicate that the obtained 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF catalyst has remarkable durability and corrosion resistance.

Due to the extra hydrolysis process, the HER kinetics of catalysts in alkaline media are often substantially slower than those in acidic media. Thus, most sophisticated catalysts (even noble metal catalysts) that work well in acidic media lose a significant amount of performance in alkaline media [70]. Surprisingly, this HER performance study reveals that the HER electrocatalytic activity of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF in alkaline electrolytes does not seem to be lower than in acidic electrolytes. In comparison to other literatures reports [10,22,48,54,71], 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF has excellent electrocatalytic performance in a wide pH range, not just at a single pH. Moreover, this work highlights the regulation of bimetallic phosphide active sites through the addition of CDs. Consequently, this catalyst is an ideal alternative for many electrolysis applications. It is expected that future work will continue to investigate strategies for improving the intrinsic catalytic activity of this electrocatalyst.

In conclusion, a self-supported CDs-oriented strategy was combined with a facile hydrothermal treatment and phosphorization process to control the growth of distinctive NiCoP microspheres with urchin-like structures on NF. The prepared 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF catalyst exhibits rapid charge transport, a large surface area, hydrophilic surfaces, and remarkable electrolytic HER performance in different electrolytes with a wide pH range. The interactions between the CDs and bimetallic phosphide provide this catalyst with more active sites, while the heteroatom doping of CDs further induces electron transfer. These factors contribute to the superior catalytic performance of 0.5CDs-NiCoP/NF, which exhibits strong potential for application as a wide-pH water electrolysis catalyst for hydrogen production.

Haixing Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xu Jiang: Data curation. Mengjing Jin: Formal analysis. Jianqiao Song: Investigation. Muxuan Li: Investigation. Jinyuan Zhou: Supervision. Xiaojun Pan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2022-08), the Basic Research Project of Qinghai Province (No.2022-ZJ-762), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11975114 and 12275114).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.08.155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
